note_data.title: Work and power
keyPoints: **Work** is done when a force moves an object through a distance in the direction of the force
**Work** is calculated using W = F × d where F is force in newtons and d is distance in metres
Work is measured in **joules (J)** where 1 joule = 1 newton × 1 metre
**No work is done** when force is applied but object does not move, or when force is perpendicular to direction of motion
**Power** is the rate of doing work or the rate of energy transfer
**Power** is calculated using P = W/t where W is work done in joules and t is time in seconds
Power is measured in **watts (W)** where 1 watt = 1 joule per second
Power can also be calculated using **P = F × v** where F is force and v is velocity
When calculating work, only consider the **component of force in the direction of motion**

definitions.definition: Work is done when a force moves an object through a distance in the direction of the force
Power is the rate of doing work or the rate of energy transfer
The joule is the unit of work done when a force of 1 newton moves an object through a distance of 1 metre
The watt is the unit of power equal to 1 joule of work done per second

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Show the formula, substitute values with units, then give final answer with correct unit.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'

modelAnswer.question: A crane lifts a load of 500 N through a vertical height of 12 m in 8 seconds. Calculate (a) the work done (b) the power developed by the crane.
modelAnswer.answer:
(a) Work done = force × distance
W = F × d
W = 500 N × 12 m
W = 6000 J

(b) Power = work done / time
P = W / t
P = 6000 J / 8 s
P = 750 W
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct formula W = F × d
Mark 3: Correct formula P = W / t

commonMistakes: Never confuse work and power - work is energy transferred, power is the rate of energy transfer
Students often forget to use the distance moved in the direction of force, not total distance travelled
Always include correct units - work in joules (J), power in watts (W), never mix up the units
Never say work is done when an object moves in a circle at constant speed with centripetal force - no work is done as force is perpendicular to motion
Students often use time incorrectly - power = work/time, not work × time
examinerTips: This topic appears almost every year in Paper 2, especially calculation questions
Always show the formula first, then substitute values with units for full marks
Remember that pushing against a wall does no work because there is no movement
Power questions often link to efficiency calculations in Pakistani context like Tarbela Dam hydroelectric power
formulas: Efficiency = (useful power output / total power input) × 100%
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Force-displacement diagram showing force F acting on an object moving distance d in the same direction, with work done = area under force-distance graph
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q5 (3 marks) — they asked students to determine the magnitude of braking force when work done = 250 J over distance = 18 m
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — students had to identify which movement of a suitcase around an airport system involved the greatest net work done
This concept was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q7 (4 marks) — candidates calculated power output of a motor lifting a load at constant velocity
This topic was tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q6 (5 marks) — students analyzed work-energy transfers in a roller coaster scenario with multiple energy conversions

workedExample.question: A braking force brings a car to rest. The work done against friction is 250 J over a distance of 18 m. Calculate the magnitude of the braking force. (3 marks)
steps: Step 1: Write the work formula: Work done = Force × distance
Step 2: Rearrange to find force: Force = Work done ÷ distance
Step 3: Substitute values: Force = 250 J ÷ 18 m
workedExample.answer: The magnitude of the braking force is 14 N
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct formula, 1 mark for correct substitution, 1 mark for final answer with appropriate significant figures

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused work done BY a force with work done AGAINST a force' — always check the direction of force relative to motion when calculating work
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to convert units before calculation' — always ensure force is in Newtons, distance in meters, and time in seconds before applying formulas
Examiner Report 2024: 'Candidates often mixed up instantaneous power and average power' — remember that P = Fv gives instantaneous power only when velocity is constant
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw a force-displacement diagram showing: (1) Object with force arrow clearly labeled with magnitude and direction, (2) Displacement arrow showing distance moved, (3) Angle θ between force and displacement vectors if force is not parallel to motion, (4) Clear labels for all quantities including units. The diagram should demonstrate that work = F × s × cos θ, with special cases when θ = 0° (maximum work) and θ = 90° (zero work).
note_data.quickRecap: Work = Force × distance (when parallel), measured in Joules. Power = Work ÷ time or Force × velocity, measured in Watts. Work transfers energy between different forms. Only the component of force in the direction of motion does work - forces perpendicular to motion do zero work.
